content.text:
The combined figure works. A couple of things to improve:

1. The legend is getting crowded with 7 items — the color mapping reuses blue/red/green across panels which makes the legend confusing
2. The "Discussed politics with coworkers" label is cut off in the legend

Let me refine the legend approach — since colors repeat across facets, I should clarify which items belong to which panel.

input.old_string:
p <- ggplot(res, aes(x = pct, y = group, color = dv)) +
  geom_pointrange(aes(xmin = lo, xmax = hi),
    size = 0.5, linewidth = 0.7,
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.6)) +
  geom_text(aes(label = paste0(round(pct), "%")),
    vjust = -1.3, size = 2.8, family = "poppins", show.legend = FALSE,
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.6)) +
  facet_wrap(~ panel, ncol = 1, scales = "free_x") +
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, 100), breaks = seq(0, 100, 20),
                     labels = paste0(seq(0, 100, 20), "%")) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c(
    "Expressing opinions online"           = "#1A4AAD",
    "Responding to disagreeable posts"      = "#D71B1E",
    "Discussing politics at work/school"    = "#229A44",
    "Posted own political views on SM"      = "#1A4AAD",
    "Shared political news on SM"           = "#D71B1E",
    "Discussed politics with people online" = "#229A44",
    "Discussed politics with coworkers"     = "#FF8200"
  )) +
  labs(
    title = "Political expression by gender identity",
    subtitle = str_wrap("Predicted % from OLS controlling for age, political interest, daily SM use, education, region, and survey wave", width = 80),
    x = NULL, y = NULL,
    caption = str_wrap(paste0(
      "MEO | 17 tracking surveys, Jul 2024 \u2013 Feb 2026 | ",
      "Cisgender N = ", format(sum(analysis$gender_minority == 0), big.mark = ","),
      ", Non-binary/Trans N = ", format(sum(analysis$gender_minority == 1), big.mark = ","),
      " | Average predictions at observed covariate values | Wave-clustered SEs via simulation"
    ), width = 100)
  ) +
  theme_meo +
  theme(
    panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
    plot.title = element_text(size = 12),
    strip.text = element_text(face = "bold", size = 10),
    legend.position = "bottom",
    legend.text = element_text(size = 7.5),
    panel.spacing = unit(1, "lines")
  ) +
  guides(color = guide_legend(nrow = 2))

# Create short labels for the legend, unique per panel
res$dv_short <- case_when(
  res$dv == "Expressing opinions online"           ~ "Express opinions online",
  res$dv == "Responding to disagreeable posts"      ~ "Respond to disagreeable posts",
  res$dv == "Discussing politics at work/school"    ~ "Discuss politics at work/school",
  res$dv == "Posted own political views on SM"      ~ "Post own views on SM",
  res$dv == "Shared political news on SM"           ~ "Share political news on SM",
  res$dv == "Discussed politics with people online" ~ "Discuss politics online",
  res$dv == "Discussed politics with coworkers"     ~ "Discuss politics with coworkers"
)

# Separate plots for each panel to avoid legend confusion
res_comfort <- res %>% filter(panel == "Comfort with political expression")
res_express <- res %>% filter(panel == "Political expression")

p_comfort <- ggplot(res_comfort, aes(x = pct, y = group, color = dv_short)) +
  geom_pointrange(aes(xmin = lo, xmax = hi),
    size = 0.5, linewidth = 0.7,
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.5)) +
  geom_text(aes(label = paste0(round(pct), "%")),
    vjust = -1.3, size = 2.8, family = "poppins", show.legend = FALSE,
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.5)) +
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, 80), breaks = seq(0, 80, 20),
                     labels = paste0(seq(0, 80, 20), "%")) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c(
    "Express opinions online"           = "#1A4AAD",
    "Respond to disagreeable posts"     = "#D71B1E",
    "Discuss politics at work/school"   = "#229A44"
  )) +
  labs(title = "Comfort with political expression",
       subtitle = "% who feel at least somewhat comfortable",
       x = NULL, y = NULL, color = NULL) +
  theme_meo +
  theme(
    panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
    plot.title = element_text(size = 11, face = "bold"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 9),
    legend.position = "bottom",
    legend.text = element_text(size = 7.5)
  ) +
  guides(color = guide_legend(nrow = 1))

p_express <- ggplot(res_express, aes(x = pct, y = group, color = dv_short)) +
  geom_pointrange(aes(xmin = lo, xmax = hi),
    size = 0.5, linewidth = 0.7,
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.6)) +
  geom_text(aes(label = paste0(round(pct), "%")),
    vjust = -1.3, size = 2.8, family = "poppins", show.legend = FALSE,
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.6)) +
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, 80), breaks = seq(0, 80, 20),
                     labels = paste0(seq(0, 80, 20), "%")) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c(
    "Post own views on SM"              = "#1A4AAD",
    "Share political news on SM"        = "#D71B1E",
    "Discuss politics online"           = "#229A44",
    "Discuss politics with coworkers"   = "#FF8200"
  )) +
  labs(title = "Political expression",
       subtitle = "% who did each at least once in the past month",
       x = NULL, y = NULL, color = NULL) +
  theme_meo +
  theme(
    panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
    plot.title = element_text(size = 11, face = "bold"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 9),
    legend.position = "bottom",
    legend.text = element_text(size = 7.5)
  ) +
  guides(color = guide_legend(nrow = 1))

# Combine with patchwork
library(patchwork)

p <- p_comfort / p_express +
  plot_annotation(
    title = "Political expression by gender identity",
    subtitle = str_wrap("Predicted % from OLS controlling for age, political interest, daily SM use, education, region, and survey wave", width = 90),
    caption = str_wrap(paste0(
      "MEO | 17 tracking surveys, Jul 2024 \u2013 Feb 2026 | ",
      "Cisgender N = ", format(sum(analysis$gender_minority == 0), big.mark = ","),
      ", Non-binary/Trans N = ", format(sum(analysis$gender_minority == 1), big.mark = ","),
      " | Average predictions at observed covariate values | Wave-clustered SEs via simulation"
    ), width = 100),
    theme = theme_meo + theme(
      plot.title = element_text(size = 13, face = "bold"),
      plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 9)
    )
  )

toolUseResult.originalFile:
source("C:/Users/math_/.claude/skills/survey-search/scripts/survey_utils.R")
source("C:/Users/math_/Dropbox/PC/Documents/GitHub/ai_interns/templates/meo_theme.R")
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(lmtest)
library(sandwich)
library(stringr)

# ── 1. Load and prepare data ────────────────────────────────────────────────

catalogue <- load_catalogue()
data <- read.csv("C:/Users/math_/Dropbox/PC/Documents/GitHub/trans_incident/harmonized_data.csv",
                 fileEncoding = "UTF-8-BOM")
data <- clean_survey_data(data, catalogue)
data <- weight_survey_data(data, catalogue, method = "rake")

data <- data %>%
  mutate(
    # Comfort DVs: binary (at least somewhat comfortable = 0-4 scale, >=2)
    dv_comfort_online  = as.integer(as.numeric(pol_speech_1) >= 2),
    dv_comfort_respond = as.integer(as.numeric(pol_speech_2) >= 2),
    dv_comfort_work    = as.integer(as.numeric(pol_speech_3) >= 2),
    # Expression DVs: binary (did at least once in past month)
    dv_post            = as.integer(as.numeric(pol_behav_3) >= 1),
    dv_share           = as.integer(as.numeric(pol_behav_2) >= 1),
    dv_talk_online     = as.integer(as.numeric(pol_talk_3) >= 1),
    dv_talk_coworkers  = as.integer(as.numeric(pol_talk_2) >= 1),
    # Gender identity
    gender_identity = as.numeric(gender1),
    gender_group = case_when(
      gender_identity == 0 ~ "Man",
      gender_identity == 1 ~ "Woman",
      gender_identity %in% c(2, 3) ~ "Non-binary / Other",
      TRUE ~ NA_character_
    ),
    is_trans = case_when(
      as.numeric(gender2) == 1 ~ 1L,
      as.numeric(gender2) == 0 ~ 0L,
      TRUE ~ NA_integer_
    ),
    gender_minority = case_when(
      gender_group == "Non-binary / Other" | is_trans == 1 ~ 1L,
      gender_group %in% c("Man", "Woman") & (is_trans == 0 | is.na(is_trans)) ~ 0L,
      TRUE ~ NA_integer_
    ),
    province_num = as.numeric(province),
    region = case_when(
      province_num %in% c(4, 5, 7, 10) ~ "Atlantic",
      province_num == 11 ~ "Quebec",
      province_num == 9 ~ "Ontario",
      province_num == 2 ~ "British Columbia",
      province_num %in% c(1, 3, 12) ~ "Prairies",
      TRUE ~ NA_character_
    ),
    age_num = as.numeric(age),
    pol_interest = as.numeric(pol_intrst_1),
    educ_num = as.numeric(educ),
    wave = factor(survey_name)
  )

# SM daily use
sm_old <- c("socialUse_1","socialUse_2","socialUse_3","socialUse_4",
            "socialUse_5","socialUse_6","socialUse_8")
sm_new <- c("socmed_use_1","socmed_use_2","socmed_use_3","socmed_use_4",
            "socmed_use_5","socmed_use_6","socmed_use_9")
sm_old <- sm_old[sm_old %in% names(data)]
sm_new <- sm_new[sm_new %in% names(data)]

data <- data %>%
  rowwise() %>%
  mutate(
    sm_old_daily = {
      vals <- suppressWarnings(as.numeric(c_across(all_of(sm_old))))
      if (all(is.na(vals))) NA_integer_ else as.integer(max(vals, na.rm = TRUE) >= 5)
    },
    sm_new_daily = {
      vals <- suppressWarnings(as.numeric(c_across(all_of(sm_new))))
      if (all(is.na(vals))) NA_integer_ else as.integer(max(vals, na.rm = TRUE) >= 3)
    }
  ) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  mutate(sm_daily = coalesce(sm_old_daily, sm_new_daily))

# Analysis sample
analysis <- data %>%
  filter(!is.na(gender_minority), !is.na(age_num), !is.na(region),
         !is.na(pol_interest), !is.na(sm_daily), !is.na(educ_num))
cat("Analysis N:", nrow(analysis), "\n")
cat("Gender minority:", sum(analysis$gender_minority == 1), "\n")
cat("Cisgender:", sum(analysis$gender_minority == 0), "\n")

# ── 2. Fit models and compute predicted probabilities ────────────────────────

dv_info <- list(
  # Panel A: Comfort (% at least somewhat comfortable)
  list(var = "dv_comfort_online",  label = "Expressing opinions online",      panel = "Comfort with political expression"),
  list(var = "dv_comfort_respond", label = "Responding to disagreeable posts", panel = "Comfort with political expression"),
  list(var = "dv_comfort_work",    label = "Discussing politics at work/school", panel = "Comfort with political expression"),
  # Panel B: Expression (% who did at least once)
  list(var = "dv_post",            label = "Posted own political views on SM",      panel = "Political expression"),
  list(var = "dv_share",           label = "Shared political news on SM",           panel = "Political expression"),
  list(var = "dv_talk_online",     label = "Discussed politics with people online", panel = "Political expression"),
  list(var = "dv_talk_coworkers",  label = "Discussed politics with coworkers",     panel = "Political expression")
)

results <- list()

for (d in dv_info) {
  dv <- d$var
  label <- d$label
  panel <- d$panel

  df_model <- analysis %>% filter(!is.na(.data[[dv]]))

  f <- as.formula(paste(dv, "~ gender_minority + age_num + pol_interest + sm_daily +
                         educ_num + factor(region) + factor(wave)"))
  m <- lm(f, data = df_model, weights = wt)
  vcov_cl <- vcovCL(m, cluster = df_model$wave)

  for (gm in c(0, 1)) {
    newdata <- df_model
    newdata$gender_minority <- gm
    preds <- predict(m, newdata = newdata)
    avg_pred <- weighted.mean(preds, df_model$wt)

    set.seed(42)
    coef_draws <- MASS::mvrnorm(1000, coef(m), vcov_cl)
    X <- model.matrix(f, data = newdata)
    pred_draws <- X %*% t(coef_draws)
    avg_draws <- apply(pred_draws, 2, function(p) weighted.mean(p, df_model$wt))
    se_pred <- sd(avg_draws)

    results[[length(results) + 1]] <- data.frame(
      panel = panel,
      dv = label,
      group = ifelse(gm == 1, "Non-binary/Trans", "Cisgender"),
      pct = avg_pred * 100,
      lo = (avg_pred - 1.96 * se_pred) * 100,
      hi = (avg_pred + 1.96 * se_pred) * 100,
      stringsAsFactors = FALSE
    )
  }
}

res <- bind_rows(results)

res$group <- factor(res$group, levels = c("Non-binary/Trans", "Cisgender"))
res$panel <- factor(res$panel, levels = c("Comfort with political expression", "Political expression"))
res$dv <- factor(res$dv, levels = c(
  "Expressing opinions online",
  "Responding to disagreeable posts",
  "Discussing politics at work/school",
  "Posted own political views on SM",
  "Shared political news on SM",
  "Discussed politics with people online",
  "Discussed politics with coworkers"
))

cat("\nPredicted probabilities:\n")
print(res %>% select(panel, dv, group, pct, lo, hi) %>% mutate(across(pct:hi, ~round(., 1))))

# ── 3. Plot ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

p <- ggplot(res, aes(x = pct, y = group, color = dv)) +
  geom_pointrange(aes(xmin = lo, xmax = hi),
    size = 0.5, linewidth = 0.7,
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.6)) +
  geom_text(aes(label = paste0(round(pct), "%")),
    vjust = -1.3, size = 2.8, family = "poppins", show.legend = FALSE,
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.6)) +
  facet_wrap(~ panel, ncol = 1, scales = "free_x") +
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, 100), breaks = seq(0, 100, 20),
                     labels = paste0(seq(0, 100, 20), "%")) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c(
    "Expressing opinions online"           = "#1A4AAD",
    "Responding to disagreeable posts"      = "#D71B1E",
    "Discussing politics at work/school"    = "#229A44",
    "Posted own political views on SM"      = "#1A4AAD",
    "Shared political news on SM"           = "#D71B1E",
    "Discussed politics with people online" = "#229A44",
    "Discussed politics with coworkers"     = "#FF8200"
  )) +
  labs(
    title = "Political expression by gender identity",
    subtitle = str_wrap("Predicted % from OLS controlling for age, political interest, daily SM use, education, region, and survey wave", width = 80),
    x = NULL, y = NULL,
    caption = str_wrap(paste0(
      "MEO | 17 tracking surveys, Jul 2024 \u2013 Feb 2026 | ",
      "Cisgender N = ", format(sum(analysis$gender_minority == 0), big.mark = ","),
      ", Non-binary/Trans N = ", format(sum(analysis$gender_minority == 1), big.mark = ","),
      " | Average predictions at observed covariate values | Wave-clustered SEs via simulation"
    ), width = 100)
  ) +
  theme_meo +
  theme(
    panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
    plot.title = element_text(size = 12),
    strip.text = element_text(face = "bold", size = 10),
    legend.position = "bottom",
    legend.text = element_text(size = 7.5),
    panel.spacing = unit(1, "lines")
  ) +
  guides(color = guide_legend(nrow = 2))

save_plot(p, "C:/Users/math_/Dropbox/PC/Documents/GitHub/trans_incident/figures/combined_expression",
          width = 8, height = 7)
cat("\nPlot saved.\n")

content.text:
The combined figure is ready at `figures/combined_expression.png`. It has two panels:

- **Top panel** — Comfort with political expression: % who feel at least somewhat comfortable (3 DVs, converted from the 1-5 scale to binary ≥ somewhat comfortable)
- **Bottom panel** — Political expression: % who did each at least once in the past month (4 DVs)

Both use predicted percentages from OLS models with the same controls (age, political interest, daily SM use, education, region, survey wave). Each panel has its own clean legend. The Non-binary/Trans group shows higher rates across nearly all measures compared to Cisgender respondents, except "Discuss politics with coworkers" which is roughly equal.

message.content: Include the information in the overall title and subtitle in the caption/footnote and remove the main title and subtitle. Also increase the height of the figure so the points and % don't overlap.

    results[[length(results) + 1]] <- data.frame(
      panel = panel,

}

toolUseResult.originalFile:
source("C:/Users/math_/.claude/skills/survey-search/scripts/survey_utils.R")
source("C:/Users/math_/Dropbox/PC/Documents/GitHub/ai_interns/templates/meo_theme.R")
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(lmtest)
library(sandwich)
library(stringr)

# ── 1. Load and prepare data ────────────────────────────────────────────────

catalogue <- load_catalogue()
data <- read.csv("C:/Users/math_/Dropbox/PC/Documents/GitHub/trans_incident/harmonized_data.csv",
                 fileEncoding = "UTF-8-BOM")
data <- clean_survey_data(data, catalogue)
data <- weight_survey_data(data, catalogue, method = "rake")

data <- data %>%
  mutate(
    # Comfort DVs: binary (at least somewhat comfortable = 0-4 scale, >=2)
    dv_comfort_online  = as.integer(as.numeric(pol_speech_1) >= 2),
    dv_comfort_respond = as.integer(as.numeric(pol_speech_2) >= 2),
    dv_comfort_work    = as.integer(as.numeric(pol_speech_3) >= 2),
    # Expression DVs: binary (did at least once in past month)
    dv_post            = as.integer(as.numeric(pol_behav_3) >= 1),
    dv_share           = as.integer(as.numeric(pol_behav_2) >= 1),
    dv_talk_online     = as.integer(as.numeric(pol_talk_3) >= 1),
    dv_talk_coworkers  = as.integer(as.numeric(pol_talk_2) >= 1),
    # Gender identity
    gender_identity = as.numeric(gender1),
    gender_group = case_when(
      gender_identity == 0 ~ "Man",
      gender_identity == 1 ~ "Woman",
      gender_identity %in% c(2, 3) ~ "Non-binary / Other",
      TRUE ~ NA_character_
    ),
    is_trans = case_when(
      as.numeric(gender2) == 1 ~ 1L,
      as.numeric(gender2) == 0 ~ 0L,
      TRUE ~ NA_integer_
    ),
    gender_minority = case_when(
      gender_group == "Non-binary / Other" | is_trans == 1 ~ 1L,
      gender_group %in% c("Man", "Woman") & (is_trans == 0 | is.na(is_trans)) ~ 0L,
      TRUE ~ NA_integer_
    ),
    province_num = as.numeric(province),
    region = case_when(
      province_num %in% c(4, 5, 7, 10) ~ "Atlantic",
      province_num == 11 ~ "Quebec",
      province_num == 9 ~ "Ontario",
      province_num == 2 ~ "British Columbia",
      province_num %in% c(1, 3, 12) ~ "Prairies",
      TRUE ~ NA_character_
    ),
    age_num = as.numeric(age),
    pol_interest = as.numeric(pol_intrst_1),
    educ_num = as.numeric(educ),
    wave = factor(survey_name)
  )

# SM daily use
sm_old <- c("socialUse_1","socialUse_2","socialUse_3","socialUse_4",
            "socialUse_5","socialUse_6","socialUse_8")
sm_new <- c("socmed_use_1","socmed_use_2","socmed_use_3","socmed_use_4",
            "socmed_use_5","socmed_use_6","socmed_use_9")
sm_old <- sm_old[sm_old %in% names(data)]
sm_new <- sm_new[sm_new %in% names(data)]

data <- data %>%
  rowwise() %>%
  mutate(
    sm_old_daily = {
      vals <- suppressWarnings(as.numeric(c_across(all_of(sm_old))))
      if (all(is.na(vals))) NA_integer_ else as.integer(max(vals, na.rm = TRUE) >= 5)
    },
    sm_new_daily = {
      vals <- suppressWarnings(as.numeric(c_across(all_of(sm_new))))
      if (all(is.na(vals))) NA_integer_ else as.integer(max(vals, na.rm = TRUE) >= 3)
    }
  ) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  mutate(sm_daily = coalesce(sm_old_daily, sm_new_daily))

# Analysis sample
analysis <- data %>%
  filter(!is.na(gender_minority), !is.na(age_num), !is.na(region),
         !is.na(pol_interest), !is.na(sm_daily), !is.na(educ_num))
cat("Analysis N:", nrow(analysis), "\n")
cat("Gender minority:", sum(analysis$gender_minority == 1), "\n")
cat("Cisgender:", sum(analysis$gender_minority == 0), "\n")

# ── 2. Fit models and compute predicted probabilities ────────────────────────

dv_info <- list(
  # Panel A: Comfort (% at least somewhat comfortable)
  list(var = "dv_comfort_online",  label = "Expressing opinions online",      panel = "Comfort with political expression"),
  list(var = "dv_comfort_respond", label = "Responding to disagreeable posts", panel = "Comfort with political expression"),
  list(var = "dv_comfort_work",    label = "Discussing politics at work/school", panel = "Comfort with political expression"),
  # Panel B: Expression (% who did at least once)
  list(var = "dv_post",            label = "Posted own political views on SM",      panel = "Political expression"),
  list(var = "dv_share",           label = "Shared political news on SM",           panel = "Political expression"),
  list(var = "dv_talk_online",     label = "Discussed politics with people online", panel = "Political expression"),
  list(var = "dv_talk_coworkers",  label = "Discussed politics with coworkers",     panel = "Political expression")
)

results <- list()

for (d in dv_info) {
  dv <- d$var
  label <- d$label
  panel <- d$panel

  df_model <- analysis %>% filter(!is.na(.data[[dv]]))

  f <- as.formula(paste(dv, "~ gender_minority + age_num + pol_interest + sm_daily +
                         educ_num + factor(region) + factor(wave)"))
  m <- lm(f, data = df_model, weights = wt)
  vcov_cl <- vcovCL(m, cluster = df_model$wave)

  for (gm in c(0, 1)) {
    newdata <- df_model
    newdata$gender_minority <- gm
    preds <- predict(m, newdata = newdata)
    avg_pred <- weighted.mean(preds, df_model$wt)

    set.seed(42)
    coef_draws <- MASS::mvrnorm(1000, coef(m), vcov_cl)
    X <- model.matrix(f, data = newdata)
    pred_draws <- X %*% t(coef_draws)
    avg_draws <- apply(pred_draws, 2, function(p) weighted.mean(p, df_model$wt))
    se_pred <- sd(avg_draws)

    results[[length(results) + 1]] <- data.frame(
      panel = panel,
      dv = label,
      group = ifelse(gm == 1, "Non-binary/Trans", "Cisgender"),
      pct = avg_pred * 100,
      lo = (avg_pred - 1.96 * se_pred) * 100,
      hi = (avg_pred + 1.96 * se_pred) * 100,
      stringsAsFactors = FALSE
    )
  }
}

res <- bind_rows(results)

res$group <- factor(res$group, levels = c("Non-binary/Trans", "Cisgender"))
res$panel <- factor(res$panel, levels = c("Comfort with political expression", "Political expression"))
res$dv <- factor(res$dv, levels = c(
  "Expressing opinions online",
  "Responding to disagreeable posts",
  "Discussing politics at work/school",
  "Posted own political views on SM",
  "Shared political news on SM",
  "Discussed politics with people online",
  "Discussed politics with coworkers"
))

cat("\nPredicted probabilities:\n")
print(res %>% select(panel, dv, group, pct, lo, hi) %>% mutate(across(pct:hi, ~round(., 1))))

# ── 3. Plot ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

# Create short labels for the legend, unique per panel
res$dv_short <- case_when(
  res$dv == "Expressing opinions online"           ~ "Express opinions online",
  res$dv == "Responding to disagreeable posts"      ~ "Respond to disagreeable posts",
  res$dv == "Discussing politics at work/school"    ~ "Discuss politics at work/school",
  res$dv == "Posted own political views on SM"      ~ "Post own views on SM",
  res$dv == "Shared political news on SM"           ~ "Share political news on SM",
  res$dv == "Discussed politics with people online" ~ "Discuss politics online",
  res$dv == "Discussed politics with coworkers"     ~ "Discuss politics with coworkers"
)

# Separate plots for each panel to avoid legend confusion
res_comfort <- res %>% filter(panel == "Comfort with political expression")
res_express <- res %>% filter(panel == "Political expression")

p_comfort <- ggplot(res_comfort, aes(x = pct, y = group, color = dv_short)) +
  geom_pointrange(aes(xmin = lo, xmax = hi),
    size = 0.5, linewidth = 0.7,
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.5)) +
  geom_text(aes(label = paste0(round(pct), "%")),
    vjust = -1.3, size = 2.8, family = "poppins", show.legend = FALSE,
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.5)) +
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, 80), breaks = seq(0, 80, 20),
                     labels = paste0(seq(0, 80, 20), "%")) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c(
    "Express opinions online"           = "#1A4AAD",
    "Respond to disagreeable posts"     = "#D71B1E",
    "Discuss politics at work/school"   = "#229A44"
  )) +
  labs(title = "Comfort with political expression",
       subtitle = "% who feel at least somewhat comfortable",
       x = NULL, y = NULL, color = NULL) +
  theme_meo +
  theme(
    panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
    plot.title = element_text(size = 11, face = "bold"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 9),
    legend.position = "bottom",
    legend.text = element_text(size = 7.5)
  ) +
  guides(color = guide_legend(nrow = 1))

p_express <- ggplot(res_express, aes(x = pct, y = group, color = dv_short)) +
  geom_pointrange(aes(xmin = lo, xmax = hi),
    size = 0.5, linewidth = 0.7,
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.6)) +
  geom_text(aes(label = paste0(round(pct), "%")),
    vjust = -1.3, size = 2.8, family = "poppins", show.legend = FALSE,
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.6)) +
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, 80), breaks = seq(0, 80, 20),
                     labels = paste0(seq(0, 80, 20), "%")) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c(
    "Post own views on SM"              = "#1A4AAD",
    "Share political news on SM"        = "#D71B1E",
    "Discuss politics online"           = "#229A44",
    "Discuss politics with coworkers"   = "#FF8200"
  )) +
  labs(title = "Political expression",
       subtitle = "% who did each at least once in the past month",
       x = NULL, y = NULL, color = NULL) +
  theme_meo +
  theme(
    panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
    plot.title = element_text(size = 11, face = "bold"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 9),
    legend.position = "bottom",
    legend.text = element_text(size = 7.5)
  ) +
  guides(color = guide_legend(nrow = 1))

# Combine with patchwork
library(patchwork)

p <- p_comfort / p_express +
  plot_annotation(
    title = "Political expression by gender identity",
    subtitle = str_wrap("Predicted % from OLS controlling for age, political interest, daily SM use, education, region, and survey wave", width = 90),
    caption = str_wrap(paste0(
      "MEO | 17 tracking surveys, Jul 2024 \u2013 Feb 2026 | ",
      "Cisgender N = ", format(sum(analysis$gender_minority == 0), big.mark = ","),
      ", Non-binary/Trans N = ", format(sum(analysis$gender_minority == 1), big.mark = ","),
      " | Average predictions at observed covariate values | Wave-clustered SEs via simulation"
    ), width = 100),
    theme = theme_meo + theme(
      plot.title = element_text(size = 13, face = "bold"),
      plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 9)
    )
  )

save_plot(p, "C:/Users/math_/Dropbox/PC/Documents/GitHub/trans_incident/figures/combined_expression",
          width = 8, height = 7)
cat("\nPlot saved.\n")

1	# Canadian Surveys on Attitudes Toward Transgender People
2	
3	## Canadian Election Study (CES)
4	
5	### CES 2021 — First explicit transgender question
6	- **cps21_groupdiscrim_7**: "How much discrimination is there in Canada against... Transgender people?"
7	  - Response: A great deal / A lot / A moderate amount / A little / None at all
8	  - Same battery covers: Indigenous peoples, Black people, immigrants, women, men, gays and lesbians, white people
9	- **cps21_genderid**: Expanded gender demographics (Man / Woman / Non-binary / Other)
10	- **pes21_groups1_2**: Feeling thermometer for Gays and Lesbians (0–100)
11	- **pes21_groups1_1**: Feeling thermometer for Feminists (0–100)
12	- **pes21_feminine_1 / pes21_masculine_1**: Gender identity sliders (0–100)
13	- **pes21_newerlife**: "Newer lifestyles are contributing to the breakdown of our society" (5-point agree/disagree)
14	- Conversion therapy attitudes (PES section)
15	- Data: https://ces-eec.arts.ubc.ca/english-section/surveys/
16	
17	### CES 2025
18	- **cps25_groupdiscrim**: Same group discrimination battery fielded again (data not yet publicly available)
19	
20	### CES 2019
21	- **cps19_gender**: First wave with trans/non-binary demographic option ("Other: e.g. Trans, non-binary, two-spirit, gender-queer")
22	- **cps19_groups_therm_4**: Feeling thermometer for Feminists (0–100)
23	- **pes19_feminine / pes19_masculine**: Gender identity sliders (0–100, split sample)
24	- **Hostile sexism battery**: pes19_hostile1 ("Most women fail to appreciate all that men do for them"), pes19_hostile2 ("Women seek to gain power by getting control over men"), pes19_hostile3 ("Most women interpret innocent remarks or acts as being sexist")
25	- **Benevolent sexism battery**: pes19_benevolent1 ("Women should be cherished and protected by men"), pes19_benevolent2 ("Many women have a quality of purity that few men possess"), pes19_benevolent3 ("A good woman ought to be set on a pedestal by her man")
26	- Data: https://ces-eec.arts.ubc.ca/english-section/surveys/
27	
28	### CES 2000–2015 (recurring items)
29	- **Feminism feeling thermometer** (0–100): every wave 2000–2021
30	- **"Society would be better off if more women stayed home with their children"**: 2000–2015
31	- **"How much should be done for women"**: 2000–2015
32	
33	---
34	
35	## Perceived Discrimination / Stigma Surveys
36	
37	### Abacus Data / Women & Gender Equality Canada — 2SLGBTQI+ Communities Survey (2023)
38	- **n=3,400**, March 8–20, 2023
39	- **Q17e (Perceived stigma)**: "How much stigma, if at all, do you think each of the following 2SLGBTQI+ groups face in their daily lives in Canada?" — with "Trans" as one group. Response: A lot / Quite a bit / A little / None / Not sure
40	- **Q15c (Comfort)**: "How comfortable, or uncomfortable, are you around... People who identify as trans (their gender is not the same as the sex they were assigned at birth)" — Response: Very comfortable / Somewhat comfortable / Somewhat uncomfortable / Very uncomfortable / Not sure
41	- **Q6 (Broad discrimination)**: "How big a problem, if at all, is discrimination, stigma, and harassment in Canada for each of the following groups?" (2SLGBTQI+ among 12 groups)
42	- **Result**: 55% perceived that trans individuals face "a lot" or "quite a bit" of stigma
43	- Report: https://epe.lac-bac.gc.ca/100/200/301/pwgsc-tpsgc/por-ef/women_gender_equality_Canada/2023/111-22-e/POR111-22-Report.html
44	- **Microdata**: Not publicly released; full report with crosstabs available
45	
46	### Angus Reid Institute — Transgender Issues (2016, 2023)
47	- **2016**: n=1,416, September 2016. 85% say trans people face discrimination in daily lives
48	- **2023**: n=3,016 + 322 non-binary/trans booster, July 2023. 71% say trans people face "heavy discrimination"; 64% say increasing acceptance is a sign of social progress
49	- Also asked about comfort with trans children, hormone therapy, sports, washroom access, pronouns
50	- Reports: https://angusreid.org/transgender-issues/ and https://angusreid.org/canada-culture-wars-gender-and-trans-issues/
51	- **Microdata**: Not publicly available
52	
53	### Ipsos — LGBT+ Pride Global Survey (annual 2016–2025)
54	- Canada sample ~1,000 per wave
55	- 2023: 67% globally agree trans people face "at least a fair amount of discrimination"; 76% agree they should be protected
56	- 2024: Found declining Canadian support on some measures
57	- 2018 dedicated survey: Asked about attitudes toward "people who dress and live as one sex even though they were born another"
58	- Reports: https://www.ipsos.com/en/global-attitudes-toward-transgender-people
59	- 2024 report: https://www.ipsos.com/sites/default/files/ct/news/documents/2024-05/Pride%20Report%20FINAL_0.pdf
60	- **Microdata**: Not publicly available
61	
62	### OHRC "Taking the Pulse" (2017) — Ontario only
63	- **n=1,501**, early 2017
64	- **Q8 (Feelings)**: Rate feelings toward "Transgender people" — Very positive / Somewhat positive / Neutral / Somewhat negative / Very negative
65	- **Q9 (Accommodations)**: Does Ontario do "too much, right amount, or not enough" accommodating transgender people's needs?
66	- **Q3 (Discrimination reasons)**: "Being transgender" as a listed reason for discrimination
67	- **Result**: Only 46% of respondents had positive feelings toward trans people
68	- Report: https://www3.ohrc.on.ca/en/taking-pulse-peoples-opinions-human-rights-ontario/findings
69	- Questionnaire: https://www.ohrc.on.ca/en/appendix-b-survey-questionnaire-taking-the-pulse
70	
71	### Williams Institute / Ipsos — 23-Country Survey (2016)
72	- **n=17,105** across 23 countries including Canada, July–August 2016
73	- Support for trans rights/anti-discrimination protections; Canada scored above 67 (high support tier)
74	- Report: https://williamsinstitute.law.ucla.edu/publications/trans-rights-23-country-survey/
75	- PDF: https://williamsinstitute.law.ucla.edu/wp-content/uploads/Public-Opinion-Trans-23-Countries-Dec-2016.pdf
76	- **Microdata**: Not publicly available
77	
78	---
79	
80	## Threats, Violence & Discrimination Experiences

file.content:
# Canadian Surveys on Attitudes Toward Transgender People

## Canadian Election Study (CES)

### CES 2021 — First explicit transgender question
- **cps21_groupdiscrim_7**: "How much discrimination is there in Canada against... Transgender people?"
  - Response: A great deal / A lot / A moderate amount / A little / None at all
  - Same battery covers: Indigenous peoples, Black people, immigrants, women, men, gays and lesbians, white people
- **cps21_genderid**: Expanded gender demographics (Man / Woman / Non-binary / Other)
- **pes21_groups1_2**: Feeling thermometer for Gays and Lesbians (0–100)
- **pes21_groups1_1**: Feeling thermometer for Feminists (0–100)
- **pes21_feminine_1 / pes21_masculine_1**: Gender identity sliders (0–100)
- **pes21_newerlife**: "Newer lifestyles are contributing to the breakdown of our society" (5-point agree/disagree)
- Conversion therapy attitudes (PES section)
- Data: https://ces-eec.arts.ubc.ca/english-section/surveys/

### CES 2025
- **cps25_groupdiscrim**: Same group discrimination battery fielded again (data not yet publicly available)

### CES 2019
- **cps19_gender**: First wave with trans/non-binary demographic option ("Other: e.g. Trans, non-binary, two-spirit, gender-queer")
- **cps19_groups_therm_4**: Feeling thermometer for Feminists (0–100)
- **pes19_feminine / pes19_masculine**: Gender identity sliders (0–100, split sample)
- **Hostile sexism battery**: pes19_hostile1 ("Most women fail to appreciate all that men do for them"), pes19_hostile2 ("Women seek to gain power by getting control over men"), pes19_hostile3 ("Most women interpret innocent remarks or acts as being sexist")
- **Benevolent sexism battery**: pes19_benevolent1 ("Women should be cherished and protected by men"), pes19_benevolent2 ("Many women have a quality of purity that few men possess"), pes19_benevolent3 ("A good woman ought to be set on a pedestal by her man")
- Data: https://ces-eec.arts.ubc.ca/english-section/surveys/

### CES 2000–2015 (recurring items)
- **Feminism feeling thermometer** (0–100): every wave 2000–2021
- **"Society would be better off if more women stayed home with their children"**: 2000–2015
- **"How much should be done for women"**: 2000–2015

---

## Perceived Discrimination / Stigma Surveys

### Abacus Data / Women & Gender Equality Canada — 2SLGBTQI+ Communities Survey (2023)
- **n=3,400**, March 8–20, 2023
- **Q17e (Perceived stigma)**: "How much stigma, if at all, do you think each of the following 2SLGBTQI+ groups face in their daily lives in Canada?" — with "Trans" as one group. Response: A lot / Quite a bit / A little / None / Not sure
- **Q15c (Comfort)**: "How comfortable, or uncomfortable, are you around... People who identify as trans (their gender is not the same as the sex they were assigned at birth)" — Response: Very comfortable / Somewhat comfortable / Somewhat uncomfortable / Very uncomfortable / Not sure
- **Q6 (Broad discrimination)**: "How big a problem, if at all, is discrimination, stigma, and harassment in Canada for each of the following groups?" (2SLGBTQI+ among 12 groups)
- **Result**: 55% perceived that trans individuals face "a lot" or "quite a bit" of stigma
- Report: https://epe.lac-bac.gc.ca/100/200/301/pwgsc-tpsgc/por-ef/women_gender_equality_Canada/2023/111-22-e/POR111-22-Report.html
- **Microdata**: Not publicly released; full report with crosstabs available

### Angus Reid Institute — Transgender Issues (2016, 2023)
- **2016**: n=1,416, September 2016. 85% say trans people face discrimination in daily lives
- **2023**: n=3,016 + 322 non-binary/trans booster, July 2023. 71% say trans people face "heavy discrimination"; 64% say increasing acceptance is a sign of social progress
- Also asked about comfort with trans children, hormone therapy, sports, washroom access, pronouns
- Reports: https://angusreid.org/transgender-issues/ and https://angusreid.org/canada-culture-wars-gender-and-trans-issues/
- **Microdata**: Not publicly available

### Ipsos — LGBT+ Pride Global Survey (annual 2016–2025)
- Canada sample ~1,000 per wave
- 2023: 67% globally agree trans people face "at least a fair amount of discrimination"; 76% agree they should be protected
- 2024: Found declining Canadian support on some measures
- 2018 dedicated survey: Asked about attitudes toward "people who dress and live as one sex even though they were born another"
- Reports: https://www.ipsos.com/en/global-attitudes-toward-transgender-people
- 2024 report: https://www.ipsos.com/sites/default/files/ct/news/documents/2024-05/Pride%20Report%20FINAL_0.pdf
- **Microdata**: Not publicly available

### OHRC "Taking the Pulse" (2017) — Ontario only
- **n=1,501**, early 2017
- **Q8 (Feelings)**: Rate feelings toward "Transgender people" — Very positive / Somewhat positive / Neutral / Somewhat negative / Very negative
- **Q9 (Accommodations)**: Does Ontario do "too much, right amount, or not enough" accommodating transgender people's needs?
- **Q3 (Discrimination reasons)**: "Being transgender" as a listed reason for discrimination
- **Result**: Only 46% of respondents had positive feelings toward trans people
- Report: https://www3.ohrc.on.ca/en/taking-pulse-peoples-opinions-human-rights-ontario/findings
- Questionnaire: https://www.ohrc.on.ca/en/appendix-b-survey-questionnaire-taking-the-pulse

### Williams Institute / Ipsos — 23-Country Survey (2016)
- **n=17,105** across 23 countries including Canada, July–August 2016
- Support for trans rights/anti-discrimination protections; Canada scored above 67 (high support tier)
- Report: https://williamsinstitute.law.ucla.edu/publications/trans-rights-23-country-survey/
- PDF: https://williamsinstitute.law.ucla.edu/wp-content/uploads/Public-Opinion-Trans-23-Countries-Dec-2016.pdf
- **Microdata**: Not publicly available

---

## Threats, Violence & Discrimination Experiences

content.text:
Here are some thoughts for your discussion, organized by implications and limitations:

**Implications**

The online/offline divergence is the most striking pattern. Non-binary and trans Canadians are significantly more likely to express political views *online* (posting, sharing, discussing) and feel more comfortable doing so, yet the gap nearly disappears for *offline* speech (discussing politics with coworkers: 46% vs 47%). A few possible interpretations:

1. **Identity-motivated political engagement.** When policies and public discourse directly concern your identity and rights, the stakes of *not* speaking up are high. This is consistent with research on "issue publics" -- people who are personally affected by an issue are more engaged on it, regardless of their general political profile.

2. **Platform affordances.** Online spaces offer affordances that may lower the barrier for gender minorities: pseudonymity, community curation, audience selection. The fact that the comfort gap shrinks for offline contexts (work/school) -- where you cannot control your audience -- is consistent with this reading.

3. **Counter-speech despite hostility, not absence of it.** Paired with the gender ideology belief results (35% of Canadians believe schools are "indoctrinating kids with radical gender ideology"), the pattern suggests that non-binary/trans people are speaking up *in spite of* a hostile climate, not because the climate is benign. This is politically consequential: it means their voices may be disproportionately visible in online discourse relative to their population share, which could itself fuel backlash narratives.

4. **The offline exception matters.** The null finding for discussing politics with coworkers suggests that workplace/in-person settings may carry different social costs (e.g., fear of professional repercussions, lack of anonymity) that equalize behavior across groups. This has implications for workplace speech climate research.

**Limitations**

1. **Selection into surveys.** Non-binary/trans respondents who complete 17 waves of a tracking survey are likely more politically engaged than the average gender minority. The N=591 over 17 waves is not trivial, but it is a self-selected online panel, and the results may overstate the comfort and expression levels of the broader non-binary/trans population.

2. **Social desirability / identity-consistent responding.** Gender minorities who are open enough to identify as such in a survey may also be more comfortable with political expression generally. People who are closeted or less out would not appear in the non-binary/trans category at all, creating survivorship bias.

3. **Content of expression is unobserved.** The measures capture *whether* people express political views, not *what* they say or *how* they are received. Higher expression rates could coexist with high rates of harassment, pile-ons, or self-censorship on specific topics, none of which these measures capture.

4. **Cross-sectional comparison, not causal.** Even with controls, this is an associational finding. We cannot rule out that non-binary/trans respondents differ on unmeasured confounders (e.g., conscientiousness about political issues, social media literacy, community embeddedness) that drive both identity disclosure *and* political expression.

5. **Broad controls, specific mechanism.** The models control for political interest, which is itself likely on the causal pathway (being a gender minority in a politicized climate may *increase* political interest). Controlling for it may attenuate the total effect of gender minority status.

6. **Small-sample precision.** With N=591 gender minorities across 17 waves (~35 per wave on average), the wave-clustered SEs are wide for this group, and the confidence intervals for the offline measures already overlap substantially with cisgender estimates.
